---
title: "The Vancouver Cherry Blossom Marathon"
subtitle: "A Data-Driven Guide to the City's Most Spectacular Streets"
date: "2026-03-30"
categories: [general, R]
image: cherry_blossoms-night.jpg
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(spopt)
library(mapgl)
library(here)
```

```{r}
#| label: load-and-route
#| message: false
#| echo: false

top30_stops <- st_read(
  here("submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/processed/top30_streets.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
)
tsp_stops <- top30_stops %>% filter(rank <= 10)
all_streets <- st_read(
  here("submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/final/streets.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
)

coords_matrix <- st_coordinates(tsp_stops)
cost_matrix_euclidean <- as.matrix(dist(coords_matrix))

result <- route_tsp(
  tsp_stops,
  start = 1,
  end = NULL,
  cost_matrix = cost_matrix_euclidean
)

stops_ordered <- result %>%
  filter(!is.na(.visit_order)) %>%
  arrange(.visit_order) %>%
  mutate(
    label = as.character(.visit_order),
    rank_label = as.character(rank),
    google_address = paste0(street_label, ", Vancouver, BC")
  )

top30_clusters <- top30_stops %>%
  st_transform(32610) %>%
  st_buffer(500) %>%
  st_union() %>%
  st_cast("POLYGON") %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  mutate(cluster_id = row_number()) %>%
  st_transform(4326)

google_maps_url <- function(stops_sf, address_col = "google_address") {
  stops_sf[[address_col]] %>%
    URLencode(reserved = TRUE) %>%
    paste(collapse = "/") %>%
    paste0("https://www.google.com/maps/dir/", .)
}

google_url <- google_maps_url(stops_ordered)

route_preview <- stops_ordered %>%
  st_coordinates() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("X", "Y"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_combine() %>%
  st_cast("LINESTRING") %>%
  st_as_sf()

walk_km <- as.numeric(st_length(st_transform(route_preview, 32610))) / 1000
walk_miles <- walk_km * 0.621371
```

Using data from the [Vancouver Open Data Portal](https://opendata.vancouver.ca/pages/home/),
some vibes, and lots of checking
We have the Vancouver Cherry Blossom (Ultra?) Marathon!

Every spring, Vancouver transforms into one of the most breathtaking cities on earth.
Hundreds of cherry blossoms erupt in pink and white blossoms, turning ordinary streets into canopies of colour.
The season lasts only a few weeks;
The official [Vancouver Cherry Blossom Festival](https://vcbf.ca/)
runs from March 27 to April 17.

But which streets are *actually* worth visiting? And if you only had one day, could you see the best of them all in a single route?

In this part of the blog series, I'll just cover the main results.
The more technical code breakdown will be in later posts.
All of this was inspired by [Kyle Walker](https://walker-data.com/)'s
[{spopt}](https://walker-data.com/spopt-r) [route optimization code](https://walker-data.com/spopt-r/articles/routing.html) from his [LinkedIn post](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/walkerke_rstats-activity-7440044826521133057-PIhA).

If you're interested, the code for this analysis is all here: <https://github.com/chendaniely/yvr-cherry-blossoms>

## Where are Vancouver's cherry blossoms?

Not all neighborhoods are created equal when it comes to cherry blossoms.

The map below shows every individual Japanese flowering cherry tree in Vancouver's public tree inventory, overlaid on neighbourhood boundaries.
There are many different types of trees that are in the city, I hope I've properly captured the correct set of cherry blossoms,
I didn't know there were so many different kinds!

```{r}
#| label: map-all-trees
#| echo: false
#| message: false

neighbourhoods_raw <- st_read(
  here(
    "submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/original/local-area-boundary.geojson"
  ),
  quiet = TRUE
)
cherry_trees_raw <- st_read(
  here("submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/final/cherry_blossoms.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
)
cherry_trees_raw <- st_transform(cherry_trees_raw, st_crs(neighbourhoods_raw))
cherry_trees_raw <- st_join(
  cherry_trees_raw,
  neighbourhoods_raw %>% select(neighbourhood = name),
  join = st_within
)

tree_variety_names <- sort(unique(cherry_trees_raw$common_name))
tree_variety_colors <- colorRampPalette(
  c("#e879a0", "#a855f7", "#3b82f6", "#10b981", "#f59e0b", "#ef4444", "#06b6d4")
)(length(tree_variety_names))

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = neighbourhoods_raw) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "neighbourhood-borders-raw",
    source = neighbourhoods_raw,
    line_color = "#d97706",
    line_width = 1.5,
    line_opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "all-trees",
    source = cherry_trees_raw,
    circle_radius = 3,
    circle_color = match_expr(
      column = "common_name",
      values = tree_variety_names,
      stops = tree_variety_colors,
      default = "#cccccc"
    ),
    circle_opacity = 0.6,
    tooltip = concat(
      "<b>",
      get_column("common_name"),
      "</b><br/>",
      "<i>Prunus ",
      get_column("species_name"),
      " '",
      get_column("cultivar_name"),
      "'</i><br/><br/>",
      "Neighbourhood: ",
      get_column("neighbourhood"),
      "<br/>",
      "Age: ",
      number_format(get_column("age_years"), 0),
      " yrs<br/>",
      "Height: ",
      get_column("height_m"),
      " m<br/>",
      "Diameter: ",
      get_column("diameter_cm"),
      " cm"
    )
  ) %>%
  add_categorical_legend(
    legend_title = "Tree variety",
    values = tree_variety_names,
    colors = tree_variety_colors,
    patch_shape = "circle"
  )
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Finding the right trees

The results below are contingent on me finding the correct set of trees.
I've learned there are more trees other than the "Japanese Flowering Cherry" that will be blooming this season.
This was the set of trees I've found while looking for some combination of "cherry", "flowering", and "japanese".
:::

The next map shows tree *density* per neighbourhood. Smaller, denser areas tend to concentrate cherry trees along residential streets, which means more blossoms per step.
This way, if you're in a certain area, you can see more cherry blossoms as you walk around.

```{r}
#| label: map-neighbourhoods
#| echo: false
#| message: false

neighbourhoods <- st_read(
  here(
    "submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/original/local-area-boundary.geojson"
  ),
  quiet = TRUE
)
cherry_trees <- st_read(
  here("submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/final/cherry_blossoms.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
)
cherry_trees <- st_transform(cherry_trees, st_crs(neighbourhoods))

tree_counts <- st_join(cherry_trees, neighbourhoods, join = st_within) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  count(name, name = "tree_count")

neighbourhoods <- neighbourhoods %>%
  mutate(area_km2 = as.numeric(st_area(geometry)) / 1e6) %>%
  left_join(tree_counts, by = "name") %>%
  mutate(
    tree_count = replace(tree_count, is.na(tree_count), 0),
    trees_per_km2 = tree_count / area_km2,
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "<b>",
      name,
      "</b><br/>",
      "Trees per km²: ",
      round(trees_per_km2, 1),
      "<br/>",
      "Total trees: ",
      tree_count
    )
  )

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = neighbourhoods) %>%
  add_fill_layer(
    id = "neighbourhood-density",
    source = neighbourhoods,
    fill_color = interpolate(
      column = "trees_per_km2",
      values = c(
        0,
        median(neighbourhoods$trees_per_km2),
        max(neighbourhoods$trees_per_km2)
      ),
      stops = c("#f7fcf0", "#41ab5d", "#00441b"),
      na_color = "#cccccc"
    ),
    fill_opacity = 0.75,
    tooltip = "tooltip_text"
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "neighbourhood-borders",
    source = neighbourhoods,
    line_color = "white",
    line_width = 1,
    line_opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  add_continuous_legend(
    legend_title = "Cherry trees per km²",
    values = c(0, max(neighbourhoods$trees_per_km2)),
    colors = c("#f7fcf0", "#00441b"),
    position = "bottom-right"
  )
```

The highest density neighborhood is in Mount Pleasant (where they had the Olympic Village).
But the [Vancouver Cherry Blossom Festival](https://vcbf.ca/) happens in David Lam Park in Downtown Vancouver,
one of the lowest cherry blossom density neighborhoods.
If you compare the tree locations to the neighborhood map,
you can see almost all the cherry blossoms trees are in David Lam Park, but the downtown area is massive.

## The best streets in the city

Neighbourhoods are a useful overview, but the real experience happens at street level.
We want high density areas (streets) to take our scenic pictures!

Instead of neighborhood-level, we'll look at individual street segments in Vancouver and calculate how many cherry trees line each block.
Then, similar to looking at neighborhood density, we can normalize by street length to find the streets with the highest blossom *density*.

The map below shows the top 30 streets.
The circled areas show about a 500 meter radius so if you're already in one location,
feel free to walk around to other highlighted streets.

```{r}
#| label: map-top30
#| echo: false
#| message: false

streets_sf <- st_read(
  here("submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/processed/streets_counted.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
)
streets_dense <- streets_sf %>% filter(total_trees >= 5)

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = top30_stops) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "all-streets",
    source = all_streets,
    line_color = "#dddddd",
    line_width = 1,
    line_opacity = 0.4
  ) %>%
  add_fill_layer(
    id = "clusters-fill",
    source = top30_clusters,
    fill_color = "#fde68a",
    fill_opacity = 0.25
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "clusters-border",
    source = top30_clusters,
    line_color = "#d97706",
    line_width = 1.5,
    line_opacity = 0.7
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "streets-density",
    source = streets_dense,
    line_color = interpolate(
      column = "density",
      values = c(0, median(streets_dense$density), max(streets_dense$density)),
      stops = c("#edf8e9", "#74c476", "#00441b")
    ),
    line_width = interpolate(
      column = "density",
      values = c(0, max(streets_dense$density)),
      stops = c(1.5, 6)
    ),
    line_opacity = 0.9,
    tooltip = "street_label",
    popup = concat(
      "<b>",
      get_column("street_label"),
      "</b><br/>",
      "Trees / 100m: ",
      number_format(get_column("density"), 1),
      "<br/>",
      "Total trees: ",
      get_column("total_trees")
    )
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "top30-circles",
    source = top30_stops,
    circle_radius = 10,
    circle_color = "white",
    circle_stroke_color = "#d97706",
    circle_stroke_width = 1.5,
    circle_opacity = 0.9,
    tooltip = "street_label",
    popup = concat(
      "<b>#",
      get_column("rank"),
      " — ",
      get_column("street_label"),
      "</b><br/>",
      "Trees / 100m: ",
      number_format(get_column("density"), 1),
      "<br/>",
      "Total trees: ",
      get_column("total_trees")
    )
  ) %>%
  add_symbol_layer(
    id = "top30-labels",
    source = top30_stops,
    text_field = get_column("rank_label"),
    text_size = 10,
    text_color = "#d97706",
    text_font = list("Open Sans Bold")
  )
```

What I found interesting is that even though Mount Pleasant has the highest density of cherry blossoms,
it doesn't have any of the top 30 densest streets.

:::{.callout-note}
## But what about...

Just because a particular location isn't highlighted in the top 30 doesn't mean it's not a good place to look for Cherry Blossoms.
Any of the highlighted green streets have a good density of trees for you to explore.
So if you're walking around and next to a dense street, why not take a quick detour and appreciate what Vancouver has to offer this time of year.
:::


## A day in Vancouver

Let's say you only have a day to spend, what might be a good way to see the blossoms and spend a day downtown?
Here's a balance of looking at the top street locations, where you can find trees along the way,
and also stop by and have breaks along your walk.

Here's my recommendation:

1. Start in Stanley Park by Devonian Harbour Park (#5)
2. Walk along the seawall towards Lost Lagoon (#2)
3. Either continuing walking South-West across Stanley park trails, or head out towards Denman Street for a break.
4. Walk towards Davie St to have a meal for another break and snack
5. Keep walking down Davie until you get to David Lam Park OR turn right on Thurlow Street towards the seawall towards David Lam Park
6. Keep walking down until you get to David Lam Park (#1)
7. Take the Aquabus towards Granville Island to finish your day

```{r}
#| label: map-day-route
#| echo: false
#| message: false

day_stops <- data.frame(
  stop = 1:7,
  label = c(
    "1. Devonian Harbour Park",
    "2. Lost Lagoon",
    "3. Davie St (meal)",
    "4. Thurlow Street",
    "5. Seawall",
    "6. David Lam Park",
    "7. Granville Island"
  ),
  lon = c(
    -123.133798,
    -123.137124,
    -123.141711,
    -123.131364,
    -123.136197,
    -123.125314,
    -123.134737
    ),
  lat = c(
    49.294064,
    49.294329,
    49.287177,
    49.280425,
    49.277237,
    49.272652,
    49.272579
    )
)

day_stops_sf <- st_as_sf(day_stops, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

day_route_sf <- day_stops_sf %>%
  st_combine() %>%
  st_cast("LINESTRING") %>%
  st_as_sf()

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = day_stops_sf) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "day-route",
    source = day_route_sf,
    line_color = "#7c3aed",
    line_width = 3,
    line_opacity = 0.8,
    line_dasharray = c(2, 1)
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "day-stops-bg",
    source = day_stops_sf,
    circle_radius = 14,
    circle_color = "white",
    circle_stroke_color = "#7c3aed",
    circle_stroke_width = 2,
    circle_opacity = 0.95,
    tooltip = "label"
  ) %>%
  add_symbol_layer(
    id = "day-stop-numbers",
    source = day_stops_sf,
    text_field = get_column("stop"),
    text_size = 11,
    text_color = "#7c3aed",
    text_font = list("Open Sans Bold")
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: day-route-link
#| echo: false

day_gmaps_url <- paste0(
  "https://www.google.com/maps/dir/",
  paste(paste(day_stops$lat, day_stops$lon, sep = ","), collapse = "/")
)

htmltools::HTML(sprintf(
  '<div style="text-align:center; margin: 2em 0;">
    <a href="%s" target="_blank"
       style="display:inline-block; background:#7c3aed; color:white; padding:0.8em 2em;
              border-radius:8px; font-size:1.1em; font-weight:bold; text-decoration:none;">
      🌸 Open the Day Route in Google Maps
    </a>
  </div>',
  #day_gmaps_url
  "https://www.google.com/maps/dir/49.294064,-123.133798/49.294329,-123.137124/49.2804478,-123.1313002/49.2768455,-123.1362682/49.2730191,-123.1312591/49.2709206,-123.1257702/49.272579,-123.134737/@49.2811033,-123.1399302,14.93z/data=!4m24!4m23!1m0!1m10!3m4!1m2!1d-123.1465475!2d49.2914927!3s0x54867226a8dff5af:0xa1fb870daa780b23!3m4!1m2!1d-123.1406573!2d49.2863645!3s0x5486722f349d5871:0x89cec114749c218e!1m0!1m0!1m5!3m4!1m2!1d-123.125377!2d49.272503!3s0x548673d0efed962d:0x340f0ffa0b386ec7!1m0!1m0!3e2?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDMzMC4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D"
))
```

## The Cherry Blossom Marathon

With the top 10 streets identified,
we can take
[Kyle Walker](https://walker-data.com/)'s
[{spopt}](https://walker-data.com/spopt-r) [route optimization code](https://walker-data.com/spopt-r/articles/routing.html)
code to find the most efficient order to visit all of them in a single trip.

I'm calling this the **Vancouver Cherry Blossom Marathon**(TM) - you heard it here first, folks!

The route connects the 10 densest cherry blossom streets in the city, covering **`r sprintf("%.1f km (%.1f miles)", walk_km, walk_miles)`** as the crow flies.
Whether you do it on foot, by bike, or by car, this is the definitive Vancouver spring itinerary.

```{r}
#| label: map-marathon
#| echo: false

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = top30_stops) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "all-streets",
    source = all_streets,
    line_color = "#dddddd",
    line_width = 1,
    line_opacity = 0.3
  ) %>%
  add_fill_layer(
    id = "clusters-fill",
    source = top30_clusters,
    fill_color = "#fde68a",
    fill_opacity = 0.25
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "clusters-border",
    source = top30_clusters,
    line_color = "#d97706",
    line_width = 1.5,
    line_opacity = 0.7
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "route-preview",
    source = route_preview,
    line_color = "#e11d48",
    line_width = 3,
    line_opacity = 0.85,
    line_dasharray = c(2, 1)
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "top30-circles",
    source = top30_stops,
    circle_radius = 10,
    circle_color = "white",
    circle_stroke_color = "#d97706",
    circle_stroke_width = 1.5,
    circle_opacity = 0.85,
    tooltip = "street_label"
  ) %>%
  add_symbol_layer(
    id = "top30-labels",
    source = top30_stops,
    text_field = get_column("rank_label"),
    text_size = 10,
    text_color = "#d97706",
    text_font = list("Open Sans Bold")
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "marathon-stops",
    source = stops_ordered,
    circle_radius = 13,
    circle_color = "white",
    circle_stroke_color = "#e11d48",
    circle_stroke_width = 2,
    circle_opacity = 0.95,
    tooltip = "street_label",
    popup = concat(
      "<b>Stop ",
      get_column(".visit_order"),
      " — ",
      get_column("street_label"),
      "</b><br/>",
      "Trees / 100m: ",
      number_format(get_column("density"), 1),
      "<br/>",
      "Total trees: ",
      get_column("total_trees")
    )
  ) %>%
  add_symbol_layer(
    id = "marathon-labels",
    source = stops_ordered,
    text_field = get_column("label"),
    text_size = 11,
    text_color = "#e11d48",
    text_font = list("Open Sans Bold")
  )
```

:::{.callout-note}
## Starting in David Lam Park
The more optimal path would be to start in the top left corner in Stanley Park,
but I figured why not start at the actual #1 location which is also where the Cherry Blossom Festival location is held.
:::

### The stops

```{r}
#| label: stop-table
#| echo: false

stops_ordered %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(
    Stop = .visit_order,
    Street = street_label,
    `Trees / 100m` = density,
    `Total trees` = total_trees
  ) %>%
  mutate(`Trees / 100m` = round(`Trees / 100m`, 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

## Do it yourself

Scroll to the bottom for the GPX files.

Ready to run the Cherry Blossom Marathon? I've loaded all 10 stops into Google Maps for you.

```{r}
#| label: google-link
#| echo: false

htmltools::HTML(sprintf(
  '<div style="text-align:center; margin: 2em 0;">
    <a href="%s" target="_blank"
       style="display:inline-block; background:#e11d48; color:white; padding:0.8em 2em;
              border-radius:8px; font-size:1.1em; font-weight:bold; text-decoration:none;">
      🌸 Open the Cherry Blossom Marathon in Google Maps
    </a>
  </div>',
  google_url
))
```

This route is not quite a marathon (It's about 35 km), but if you do this and check out all the trees and take photos,
you might be pretty darn close. If you really want to do the (ultra)marathon, look below at the Top-30 Route.

The green shaded areas on the maps above show clusters of great streets.
So even if you can't do all 10, any one of those zones should be worth it.

## For the cyclists: the full 30-stop Grand Tour

Can't get enough? We also mapped all 30 top streets into a bike route — split into 3 legs so Google can optimize each one. This is the definitive cherry blossom cycling tour of Vancouver.

```{r}
#| label: bike-route-data
#| echo: false
#| message: false

coords_all <- st_coordinates(top30_stops)
cost_matrix_all <- as.matrix(dist(coords_all))

result_all <- route_tsp(
  top30_stops,
  start = 1,
  end = NULL,
  cost_matrix = cost_matrix_all
)

bike_stops <- result_all %>%
  filter(!is.na(.visit_order)) %>%
  arrange(.visit_order) %>%
  mutate(
    label = as.character(.visit_order),
    rank_label = as.character(rank),
    google_address = paste0(street_label, ", Vancouver, BC"),
    leg = ceiling(row_number() / 10)
  )

google_maps_url <- function(stops_sf, address_col = "google_address") {
  stops_sf[[address_col]] %>%
    URLencode(reserved = TRUE) %>%
    paste(collapse = "/") %>%
    paste0("https://www.google.com/maps/dir/", .)
}

bike_route_line <- bike_stops %>%
  st_coordinates() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("X", "Y"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_combine() %>%
  st_cast("LINESTRING") %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  mutate(route = "bike")

bike_km <- as.numeric(st_length(st_transform(bike_route_line, 32610))) / 1000
bike_miles <- bike_km * 0.621371
```

```{r}
#| label: map-bike
#| echo: false

maplibre(style = openfreemap_style("bright"), bounds = top30_stops) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "all-streets",
    source = all_streets,
    line_color = "#dddddd",
    line_width = 1,
    line_opacity = 0.3
  ) %>%
  add_fill_layer(
    id = "clusters-fill",
    source = top30_clusters,
    fill_color = "#fde68a",
    fill_opacity = 0.25
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "clusters-border",
    source = top30_clusters,
    line_color = "#d97706",
    line_width = 1.5,
    line_opacity = 0.7
  ) %>%
  add_line_layer(
    id = "bike-route",
    source = bike_route_line,
    line_color = "#0ea5e9",
    line_width = 2.5,
    line_opacity = 0.8,
    line_dasharray = c(2, 1)
  ) %>%
  add_circle_layer(
    id = "bike-circles",
    source = bike_stops,
    circle_radius = 11,
    circle_color = "white",
    circle_stroke_color = "#0ea5e9",
    circle_stroke_width = 2,
    circle_opacity = 0.95,
    tooltip = "street_label",
    popup = concat(
      "<b>Stop ",
      get_column(".visit_order"),
      " (Leg ",
      get_column("leg"),
      ")</b><br/>",
      get_column("street_label"),
      "<br/>",
      "Trees / 100m: ",
      number_format(get_column("density"), 1)
    )
  ) %>%
  add_symbol_layer(
    id = "bike-labels",
    source = bike_stops,
    text_field = get_column("label"),
    text_size = 10,
    text_color = "#0ea5e9",
    text_font = list("Open Sans Bold")
  )
```

The full grand tour covers **`r sprintf("%.1f km (%.1f miles)", bike_km, bike_miles)`** straight-line distance — your actual ride following roads will be longer. Google Maps limits routes to 10 stops, so we've split the ride into 3 legs — open each one and chain them together for the full experience.

### The stops

```{r}
#| label: bike-stop-table
#| echo: false

bike_stops %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(
    Stop = .visit_order,
    Leg  = leg,
    Street = street_label,
    `Trees / 100m` = density,
    `Total trees` = total_trees
  ) %>%
  mutate(`Trees / 100m` = round(`Trees / 100m`, 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r}
#| label: bike-links
#| echo: false

htmltools::tagList(lapply(sort(unique(bike_stops$leg)), function(i) {
  leg_stops <- bike_stops %>% filter(leg == i)
  url <- google_maps_url(leg_stops)
  htmltools::HTML(sprintf(
    '<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1.5em 0;">
      <a href="%s" target="_blank"
         style="display:inline-block; background:#0ea5e9; color:white; padding:0.7em 1.8em;
                border-radius:8px; font-size:1em; font-weight:bold; text-decoration:none;">
        🚴 Leg %d (stops %d–%d) — Open in Google Maps
      </a>
    </div>',
    url,
    i,
    min(leg_stops$.visit_order),
    max(leg_stops$.visit_order)
  ))
}))
```

## Take the route with you

Want to follow the route on your phone or GPS watch? We've generated GPX files for every version — import into Strava, Garmin Connect, Komoot, or AllTrails and go.

:::{.callout-note}
GPX files generated with the [OSRM](https://project-osrm.org/) open-source routing engine using OpenStreetMap data.
:::

Routes are road-following, not straight-line, generated using OpenStreetMap data. The **run** and **walk** profiles use footpaths, park shortcuts, and sidewalks. The **cycling** profile prefers bike lanes and cycle paths.

### Cherry Blossom Marathon — Top 10 streets

| Activity | Distance | Download | Strava |
|---|---|---|---|
| 🏃 Walking | road-following via foot profile | [cherry-blossom-walk.gpx](../../submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/routes/cherry-blossom-walk.gpx) | [Strava](https://www.strava.com/routes/3473491710154773940) |
| 🚴 Cycling | road-following via bike profile | [cherry-blossom-bike.gpx](../../submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/routes/cherry-blossom-bike.gpx) | |

### Cherry Blossom Grand Tour — All 30 streets

| Activity | Distance | Download | Strava |
|---|---|---|---|
| 🏃 Walking | road-following via foot profile | [cherry-blossom-walk.gpx](../../submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/routes/cherry-blossom-walk.gpx) | [Strava](https://www.strava.com/routes/3473568223793890664) |
| 🚴 Cycling | road-following via bike profile | [cherry-blossom-bike.gpx](../../submodules/yvr-cherry-blossoms/data/routes/cherry-blossom-bike.gpx) | [Strava](https://www.strava.com/routes/3473485469483171920) |

## An appreciation for city planners

As I've reflected on this small analysis,
I've come to really appreciate the thoughtfulness the city planners were in planting these trees.

I did a half marathon loop around part of the course the other day,
and while I was sad not all the trees listed were in full bloom,
I did come to appreciate the intentionally placed single trees.
The fact that not all the trees bloom at the same time also means that the cherry blossom season can last much longer around the city.

![A single cherry blossom behind the Kensington Park Community Center sign on 33rd Ave and Knight Ave (2 Major Roads). Sometimes a single intensional tree makes all the difference.](kensington_park-cherry-blossom.jpg){width=50%}

Also,
it's great that so much data is published on the [Vancouver Open Data Portal](https://opendata.vancouver.ca/pages/home/),
especially the locations of the trees that the city has planted.
This entire analysis would not be possible without the city's commitment to open data.

## The blossoms don't last long. Get out there!

I'll be updating this post if I (or you) catch any technical mistakes in [the analysis](https://github.com/chendaniely/yvr-cherry-blossoms).
